title: Ask the Policy, Not the Internet
context: The team is reviewing the AI policy guidance feature for Expenses. Product wants an impressive conversational experience. Compliance is worried about fabricated answers. Customer Success wants admins to maintain a single source of truth without operational overhead. You are the solution architect. The Head of Product is chairing, and the AI lead is present.

questions.prompt: The AI Lead says: "To keep the experience smooth, if the uploaded policy does not answer a question we can let the assistant fall back to general expense best practice. Users care about helpfulness, not whether the answer came from their exact policy." Compliance looks alarmed. How should you respond?

options.text: That is the right product trade-off. A conversational assistant that says 'I do not know' too often will feel broken, so it is better to use general expenses guidance as a backstop and only flag the source when policy-specific wording happens to be available.
options.explanation: This is exactly what the requirement is trying to avoid. The assistant must answer from the uploaded or linked policy content, and if the policy does not contain the answer it should say so rather than guessing. Helpfulness does not justify inventing policy.

options.text: We should not let the assistant invent policy. The requirement is that responses are grounded in the uploaded or linked policy, and if the policy does not contain an answer the system should indicate that rather than guessing. In this feature, trustworthiness is more important than conversational fluency.
options.explanation: This is the correct response. It defines the trust boundary of the AI system: the retrieval scope is limited to the customer's uploaded policy content, and the system must indicate when the content does not contain an answer rather than falling back to general knowledge. This boundary is an architectural constraint, not just a product preference, because it determines what data the retrieval pipeline indexes and how the system handles queries with no matching content.

options.text: We can use general best practice only for low-risk questions like receipt evidence or mileage, and reserve policy grounding for higher-risk questions like financial limits. That gives us a sensible middle ground between usability and compliance.
options.explanation: The requirement does not create a low-risk exception. Once the assistant answers as though it understands the customer's policy, it must stay grounded in that policy. A selective fallback would still produce unauthorised guidance inside a policy feature.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Lead says: "We can keep the implementation simple. Modern LLMs have large enough context windows to accept a full policy document alongside each user question. We do not need a retrieval layer or a search index; we just pass the document in and let the model answer." The AI Lead looks doubtful. How should you respond?

options.text: That is the most pragmatic approach for phase one. A single document-in-context model avoids the complexity of building and maintaining a retrieval pipeline, a vector store, and a chunking strategy. If performance is acceptable and the model can answer accurately, we have eliminated an entire infrastructure layer.
options.explanation: This ignores several architectural risks. Policy documents vary significantly in size across customers and may exceed context limits. Full-document context also makes source traceability harder because the model is less precise about where in the document the answer came from. A retrieval layer is not just an optimisation; it is the mechanism that enables grounded, traceable answers from specific policy sections.

options.text: A retrieval layer is not optional here. Policy documents vary in length across customers, some will exceed practical context limits, and even where they fit, retrieval-based grounding gives us precision: the system retrieves the relevant policy sections, the model answers from those sections, and we can trace the answer back to specific source content. Without that, we lose the traceability the feature requires and the grounding quality degrades as documents get longer.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it identifies the retrieval layer as architecturally necessary rather than a nice-to-have. It connects chunked retrieval to two key requirements: source traceability and grounded answers. It also flags the variability of customer policy documents as a reason the architecture cannot assume a single document will always fit in context.

options.text: We should split the difference by passing the full document into context for small policies and switching to retrieval for larger ones. That way we avoid building retrieval infrastructure for the majority of customers who have short policies, and only pay the complexity cost when the document forces us to.
options.explanation: A dual-path architecture adds more complexity than it saves. The retrieval approach works for both small and large documents, while the full-context approach only works for small ones. Building and maintaining two answer paths means two sets of behaviour to test, two quality profiles to monitor, and inconsistent traceability across customers.

questions.prompt: The Platform Architect says: "We are already investing in a general-purpose AI assistant for the platform. The Expenses policy feature should be a prompt template on top of that shared service rather than a standalone component. One AI investment serves every module." How should you respond?

options.text: That is the right reuse strategy. A shared AI assistant reduces infrastructure duplication, gives every module access to conversational capability, and means the Expenses team does not need its own AI expertise. We can parameterise the shared service with the right policy document and expose it inside the Expenses UI.
options.explanation: This underestimates the domain-specific requirements of the policy assistant. The feature requires grounding exclusively to uploaded policy content with no fallback to general knowledge, source traceability back to specific policy sections, contextualisation using employee data, and a defined behaviour when the policy does not contain an answer. These constraints are more than a prompt template difference; they define the service's trust and safety model.

options.text: The policy assistant has domain-specific constraints that do not fit a generic assistant pattern. It must ground exclusively to uploaded policy content, refuse to answer outside that content, trace every answer back to a source section, and incorporate employee context when applying rules. Those are not prompt-template differences; they define the retrieval pipeline, the safety boundary, and the data access model. This should be a purpose-built domain service, even if it shares lower-level AI infrastructure like model access and embedding generation.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it distinguishes between reusing infrastructure and reusing service behaviour. Shared model endpoints and embedding services are sensible, but the retrieval logic, grounding rules, and trust boundary of the policy assistant are domain-specific and should be owned by the Expenses domain.

options.text: We should build the policy assistant as a standalone service now and plan to migrate it onto the shared platform assistant later once that service matures. That gives us speed now without blocking on the platform team's roadmap.
options.explanation: This creates a throwaway service on the assumption that a future platform assistant will eventually meet the domain-specific requirements. The better position is to recognise that the domain requirements justify a purpose-built service regardless of the platform timeline, while still consuming shared infrastructure where appropriate.

questions.prompt: The Sysadmin representative asks: "Do I have to keep uploading revised PDF policies every time Legal changes a line, or can I just point the system at our SharePoint policy and let that be the source? Also, what happens to old answers if I update it?" How should you respond?

options.text: For control reasons we should only support manual document uploads. Live links to SharePoint create too much unpredictability, and if the policy changes we should retroactively regenerate prior answers so the conversation history stays aligned to the latest policy version.
options.explanation: This contradicts two requirements. The feature explicitly says a SharePoint link can be uploaded and used in the same way as a document upload, and it also says that updating the policy will not reindex previous questions but will be effective from the point of upload.

options.text: The design should support either an uploaded policy document or a SharePoint link as the source of truth. When the policy is updated, the new content should apply from that point onward, but the feature does not retroactively reindex previous questions. That gives admins a maintainable source without pretending the past conversation happened against a different policy than the one in force at the time.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it addresses two architectural decisions. First, the content ingestion pipeline must support both uploaded documents and linked sources like SharePoint, treating both as valid policy sources for indexing. Second, the index lifecycle is append-forward: re-indexing applies only to new queries, preserving the integrity of prior answers against the policy version in force at the time. This avoids the complexity and auditability problems of retrospective re-indexing.

options.text: We can support both uploads and SharePoint, but if the source changes we really should rerun the index and rewrite prior answers automatically. Otherwise users may read older guidance that is now superseded, which makes the system inconsistent.
options.explanation: While the concern is understandable, it does not match the specified behaviour. The requirement explicitly says policy updates will not reindex previous questions and will instead take effect from the point of upload.

questions.prompt: The AI Lead says: "To give contextualised answers, the policy assistant should query the People system directly at inference time. If it knows the employee's grade, car allowance status, and department, it can tailor the policy response accordingly. Direct access keeps the architecture simple." How should you respond?

options.text: Direct access is the cleanest approach. The assistant needs employee data to answer accurately, so giving it read access to People eliminates an integration layer. We can scope the access to the specific fields the assistant needs and add appropriate access controls.
options.explanation: Direct coupling between the policy assistant and the People system creates a hidden runtime dependency, increases inference latency, and means the AI service needs its own read access to employee PII. It also makes the assistant harder to test and reason about because its behaviour depends on live data from another domain.

options.text: The policy assistant should receive relevant employee context as input from the calling application rather than querying People directly. The Expenses submission flow already has access to the employee's profile, so it can pass the relevant attributes when it invokes the assistant. That keeps the assistant stateless, avoids a direct dependency on People, and means the AI service does not need its own access to employee PII.
options.explanation: This is the correct response because it preserves the architectural boundary between the AI service and the People domain. The calling application is already authenticated and authorised for the employee's data, so forwarding the relevant context is simpler, faster, and more secure than giving the assistant independent data access.

options.text: We should cache a copy of the relevant employee attributes inside the policy service so it can personalise answers without calling People at query time. A nightly sync would keep the data fresh enough for policy guidance purposes.
options.explanation: Replicating employee PII into the AI service creates a second store of sensitive data with its own retention, access control, and staleness concerns. It also does not solve the coupling problem; it just makes it asynchronous. Context injection from the caller avoids all of these issues.
